content:
Damage reduces a character's hit points. If a character has zero or fewer remaining hit points, it is defeated.

  • Damage on an identity or villain is tracked by a hit point dial. If such a character takes damaged, reduce its dial by the amount of damage that it took.
  • Damage on an ally or minion is tracked by damage tokens. If such a character takes damage, place the specified value of damage tokens on the character.
  • When damage is dealt to a character, that character takes damage.
    • When the amount of damage an effect deals is modified, the amount of damage the character takes is similarly modified.
    • When the amount of damage a character takes is modified (such as by damage being prevented), the amount of damage dealt is not modified.
    • The order of resolution for effects surrounding the dealing and taking of damage are as follows:
      1. Abilities that trigger "when [character] would deal/be dealt any amount of damage..."
      2. Tough status cards.
      3. Abilities that trigger "when [character] would take any amount of damage..."
      4. Abilities that trigger "when [character] takes any amount of damage..."
      5. Placing of damage on the character.
      6. Abilities that trigger "when [character] would be defeated..."
      7. Abilities that trigger "when [character] is defeated..." (including "When Defeated" abilities).
      8. Discarding of a defeated character.
      9. Abilities that trigger "after [character] deals/is dealt/takes any amount of damage..." or "after [character] defeats/is defeated..."

• A value presented as a dash (–) indicates that value cannot be used.
  • If a card has a dash as cost, it cannot be played (only enters play through other means).
  • If a power (ATK/DEF/REC/SCH/THW) has a dash, the character cannot exhaust to use that power.
  • A dash referenced by a game step/ability is treated as an unmodifiable 0.

During step three of the villain phase, each player is dealt one facedown encounter card.

If a card ability instructs a player to be dealt an encounter card, the player takes the top card of the encounter deck and places it facedown in front of them. This card is not revealed at this time. This card is added to the queue of cards that player resolves during the villain phase.

  • If a player is dealt an encounter card during step three or four of the villain phase, the extra encounter card is added to the queue of cards that are being dealt and revealed in those same steps.

There are four main types of decks that appear in a game: the player deck, the encounter deck, the villain deck, and the main scheme deck. Certain identities or scenarios may add other decks to the game.

  • The order of cards within a deck cannot be altered unless a player is instructed to do so by a game step, game function, or card ability.

If a character has zero or fewer remaining hit points, or a side scheme has no threat on it, it is defeated. If an ally, minion, or side scheme is defeated, it is discarded.

If an identity or stage of the villain is defeated, it is removed from the game.

• During an enemy attack, a player may defend using cards they control.
  • Only one player at a time can defend against an enemy attack.
  • A hero can use basic defense power to defend (must exhaust; damage reduced by DEF, remainder dealt to that hero).
  • An ally can exhaust to defend (all damage dealt to that ally).
  • A player initiating a triggered ability labeled as a defense ('Hero Interrupt (defense)') has their identity become the defender if there is not already one; resolving a defense-labeled ability is not a basic defense and does not reduce damage by the hero's DEF unless the hero is also declared defender by the normal process; a hero does not exhaust unless specified.
  • If a player defends against an attack targeting a different player, the defending player becomes the new target.
  • If no character defends, the attack is undefended; a defending ally defeated before damage is dealt also makes the attack undefended.

Some abilities contain delayed effects specifying a future timing point or condition and dictate an effect to happen then. Delayed effects resolve automatically and immediately after their specified timing point/condition occurs, and before responses to that point may be used; they have the same timing priority as constant effects.

A delayed effect is not treated as a new triggered ability even if created by one.

Discarding is attempting to move a card from a non-discard-pile play area to a discard pile. Player cards discarded go faceup on top of the owning player's discard pile; encounter cards go faceup on top of the encounter discard pile.

  • If multiple cards are discarded from hand/play by a singular effect, order is any; from a deck, one at a time without changing order (considered simultaneous, responses resolve after all discards).
  • If a player looks at cards from the top of a deck and discards some, those are considered discarded from the top of that deck.

• A discard pile is an out-of-play area containing discarded cards.
  • Each player has their own discard pile; the encounter deck has its own.
  • Each discard pile is open information viewable at any time. Order cannot be changed unless instructed by a card ability. Shuffling a discard pile of zero cards back into a deck does not shuffle the deck.

A card is double-sided if neither side has a card back (player, villain, or encounter). If it would enter an out-of-play area other than the victory display or set-aside area, it is removed from the game.

A card with 'Standard Mode Only' and 'Expert Mode Only' sides is put into play with the appropriate side faceup based on the mode being played.

If a player is instructed to draw cards, they are drawn from the top of their deck one at a time and added to hand. Each player's hand size is checked at the end of the player phase; if over hand size, they must discard down.
